name: Drip
technique: Paint dripped from stick/brush held above canvas laid flat on floor; artist walks around or over canvas controlling height and movement speed
physics: Gravity-driven, viscosity-dependent flow, Rayleigh-Taylor instability at nozzle; Plateau-Rayleigh instability causes bead formation; flow rate Q = πr⁴ΔP/8ηL (Hagen-Poiseuille)
control: Low-Medium — direction predictable, exact form not
speed: Slow to medium — determined by viscosity
visual_result: Sinuous lines, loops, beads, variable thickness along path; thickness varies from 1–15mm depending on paint and height
layering_quality: Excellent — lines remain distinct, no mudding
emotional_association: Flow, improvisation, surrender to process, the subconscious made visible
color_stacking: Multiple colors visible as separate lines, chronological layering readable
texture_quality: Slight relief, bead texture at endpoints, raised ridges along path
variation_potential: High — varies with paint viscosity, height, speed of movement, angle of stick

name: Pour
technique: Paint poured directly from container onto canvas; canvas tilted to direct flow; used with heavily thinned paint on raw unprimed canvas to create staining effect
physics: Gravity-driven mass flow; surface tension and capillary absorption dominate on raw canvas; viscous fingering instability at flow fronts; absorption rate determines edge sharpness
control: Low — direction broadly controllable by canvas tilt, but branching paths unpredictable
speed: Slow — determined by gravity and viscosity
visual_result: Broad pools, rivers of color, stained halos at edges, soft bleeding where absorbed; no impasto; color becomes part of canvas weave
layering_quality: Moderate — colors blend where wet, stain layers can be distinct when dry between pours
emotional_association: Openness, natural forces, transparency, color as atmosphere
color_stacking: Blending and mixing at pour edges; overlapping stains show through each other
texture_quality: Flat to very low relief; texture comes from canvas weave showing through absorbed paint
variation_potential: Very High — viscosity, volume, tilt angle, canvas absorbency, drying time all affect result
repeatability: Very Low — atmospheric conditions affect absorption
scale_capability: Large to very large; intimate scale loses atmospheric effect
duration: Rapid — seconds to minutes per pour event

name: Splash/Splatter
technique: Paint thrown or flicked from brush/stick at canvas from distance; high-velocity application; body momentum transfers directly to paint mass; Pollock used a hardened brush or stick to fling loaded paint
physics: Projectile motion followed by impact splatter; Weber number We = ρv²d/σ determines splatter pattern; high We produces fine secondary droplets; impact crater and corona formation
control: Low — splatter radius and secondary droplets difficult to predict
visual_result: Central impact mark with radiating secondary droplets; star-burst patterns; fine mist of micro-droplets surrounding main mark; tails indicate direction of travel
layering_quality: Good — small marks integrate well with underlying layers
emotional_association: Energy, violence, release, explosive emotion, the gestural body
color_stacking: Fine dots visible against lower layers; creates visual noise and texture
texture_quality: Varied relief — central drops can be thick, secondary mist nearly flat
variation_potential: Very High — velocity, paint load, distance, angle all affect pattern
scale_capability: Best at medium to large scale; fine splatter reads poorly at small scale
cleanup_effort: High — micro-droplets travel far beyond intended zone

name: Sweep/Swipe
technique: Large loaded brush, palette knife, or squeegee dragged across canvas in single decisive arc or straight motion; typically involves full arm or body movement; Kline used commercial house-painting brushes up to 8 inches wide
physics: Viscous shear flow beneath brush; trailing edge cavitation leaves texture; paint compresses at leading edge of stroke and thins at trailing edge; surface roughness of brush transfers to paint
control: Medium-High — direction highly controllable, edge quality variable
speed: Fast to very fast — gesture typically made in under 2 seconds
visual_result: Bold gestural marks with directional emphasis; visible brush texture within stroke; tapered beginnings and ends; dry-brush effects at edges if paint load diminishes
layering_quality: Good — bold marks stand clearly against previous layers
emotional_association: Decisiveness, confidence, raw power, the weight of the body, urgency
color_stacking: Upper layers pick up underlying paint if wet; glazing effects possible with dry layers
texture_quality: Medium-High relief; brush hairs create fine texture within stroke; impasto at stroke beginning
variation_potential: High — brush size, paint load, speed, pressure all affect width and texture
repeatability: Medium — practiced artists achieve consistent sweep character
scale_capability: Large scale essential — small sweeps lose body-gesture quality

name: Stab/Jab
technique: Brush or palette knife pressed and twisted into canvas with short percussive or stabbing motion; paint compressed against surface; often used to build up complex paint masses in areas of compositional focus
physics: Compressive force applied normal to canvas surface; viscoelastic paint deforms under pressure and springs back partially; repeat jabbing builds impasto; brush hairs splay under pressure creating fan marks
control: Medium — location precise, exact mark shape less so
visual_result: Starburst or comma-shaped marks; heavily textured impasto; overlapping press marks creating complex paint surfaces; fan-brush splaying effects
layering_quality: Complex — stabbing breaks through previous layers and mixes; depth effect in thick paint
emotional_association: Aggression, urgency, frustration, the body's raw force, psychological intensity
color_stacking: Colors blend and mix where stabbing disturbs previous wet layers
texture_quality: Very high relief; most textured of all gestures; paint can build to 5–10mm thickness
variation_potential: High — brush type, paint load, pressure, repeat jabbing
scale_capability: Any scale; particularly effective at intimate scale for portrait/figure work

name: Scumble
technique: Dry or semi-dry brush dragged lightly over textured surface with side of bristles; paint load minimal; brush skips over valleys in surface texture, catching only peaks; often used to unify or partially obscure previous layers
physics: Selective adhesion to surface asperities; paint deposits depend on surface roughness Ra; thin paint film breaks up across low-adhesion zones; Cassie-Baxter wetting theory applies to rough surface paint contact
visual_result: Broken, irregular marks that reveal underlying layers; atmospheric haze effects; soft edges; optical color mixing with underpainting
layering_quality: Excellent for integration — creates visual dialogue between layers
emotional_association: Ambiguity, memory, atmospheric depth, time passing, the incomplete
color_stacking: Optical color mixing — upper and lower colors blend in the eye, not on canvas
texture_quality: Low relief; technique is defined by the absence of thick paint
variation_potential: Medium — paint dryness and surface texture are key variables
duration: Variable — can work large areas slowly

name: Scribble
technique: Repeated rapid circular, looping, or linear marks made with pencil, crayon, oil stick, or brush tip; Twombly famously made marks with his non-dominant hand or in reduced-light conditions to access uninhibited mark-making; continuous without lifting tool
physics: Continuous contact between tool and surface; friction and normal force determine mark character; velocity variation creates thin-to-thick transitions; graphite/wax marks depend on tool hardness and paper/canvas grain
control: Low — intentional loss of control; deliberate primitiveness
visual_result: Looping, tangled line clusters; varying density; palimpsest-like accumulations; graphite or crayon marks over paint; intimate, diaristic quality
layering_quality: Excellent — line clusters build density and depth over paint layers
emotional_association: Childhood, the unconscious, writing as drawing, memory traces, eroticism in Twombly's case
color_stacking: Linear marks over color fields; graphite adds neutral density; oil stick adds color line on paint surface
texture_quality: Very low — primarily graphic/linear; slight wax or graphite relief
variation_potential: High — tool type, pressure, speed, directionality
scale_capability: Any scale; intimate scale particularly effective
duration: Extended — can fill large areas with patient accumulation

name: Scrape
technique: Palette knife, squeegee, or putty knife dragged through wet paint to remove, displace, or redistribute; de Kooning famously scraped down whole compositions and re-worked; creates striations and reveals lower layers
physics: Bulk removal of paint above the blade edge; Bingham plastic behavior — paint must exceed yield stress to flow; blade angle determines cut depth; surface roughness of canvas grabs paint at low points
control: Medium — blade direction precise, resulting texture varies
visual_result: Striated marks revealing lower layers; straight-edged paint ridges at blade terminus; compressed paint at edges; toothed texture from canvas grain showing through thin remaining paint
layering_quality: Unique — scraping creates visual archaeology, exposing chronological layers simultaneously
emotional_association: Revision, doubt, erasure, the palimpsest of thought, working and reworking
color_stacking: Color mixing across layers as scraping displaces and blends; revelation of earlier color decisions
texture_quality: Very high — ridges, furrows, exposed canvas weave; most complex surface texture of all gestures
variation_potential: Very High — blade type, angle, pressure, paint wetness, number of layers
repeatability: Low — depends on what is beneath
cleanup_effort: Low — removed paint collected on blade

name: Flick
technique: Wrist or arm snap sends paint from loaded brush tip in a short arc to canvas; smaller and more precise than a full throw/splatter; Pollock used flicking for fine linear work within larger compositions; wrist motion key
physics: Inertial release of paint from brush tip at maximum angular velocity; paint detaches when centrifugal force exceeds surface tension; detachment velocity determines travel distance; thin streams can break into droplets mid-air
control: Low-Medium — wrist angle determines direction, velocity determines length
speed: Very fast — the flick itself is instantaneous
visual_result: Short linear marks or arcs; paint tail that thins to a point; can produce fine hair-like lines at high velocity with thin paint; directional character showing wrist rotation
layering_quality: Good — fine marks integrate without disturbing lower layers
emotional_association: Precision within chaos, calligraphic energy, the decisive moment
color_stacking: Fine lines over broad areas; creates visual punctuation against ground
texture_quality: Low relief — thin lines; slight bead at mark end
variation_potential: High — wrist speed, paint load, brush size, distance from canvas
scale_capability: Any scale; effective at medium scale for detailed surface work
duration: Instantaneous per mark; rapid succession possible

name: Wash
technique: Highly thinned paint applied with large soft brush or sponge in broad horizontal or vertical passes; watercolor-like transparency; Rothko applied multiple thin washes to build luminous color depth; often on sized but not gessoed canvas
physics: Thin Newtonian fluid spreading under gravity and capillary action; optical depth from multiple transparent layers (Beer-Lambert law); light transmission through stacked glazes produces subtractive color mixing
visual_result: Luminous, translucent color fields; soft edges; slight watermark effects at drying edges; deep optical color through layer accumulation; veil-like surface quality
layering_quality: Excellent — transparent layers build optical complexity without obscuring
emotional_association: Contemplation, transcendence, meditative stillness, spiritual depth
color_stacking: Optical glazing — multiple thin color layers create colors unmixable on palette; warm/cool luminosity
texture_quality: Very low — technique defined by flatness; slight edge thickening at wash boundaries
variation_potential: Medium — dilution ratio, brush load, number of layers
repeatability: High — most controlled and repeatable of all techniques
scale_capability: Any scale; large scale enhances immersive quality
duration: Long — wash must dry between applications for clarity

name: Drizzle
technique: Paint allowed to fall in thin stream from a small hole in container or highly loaded thin stick; finer and more controlled than drip; often used for secondary line work over established drip compositions; can be done with turkey baster or punctured can
physics: Rayleigh-Plateau instability governs stream breakup; stream diameter d ∝ (σ/ρg)^(1/2); coiling instability when stream hits horizontal surface (Kaye effect possible with shear-thinning paint); thinner streams than standard drip
control: Medium — finer lines than drip, more directional control possible
visual_result: Very fine sinuous lines, 0.5–3mm width; fine beading; can produce near-continuous fine lines without bead formation if viscosity is right; writing-like quality
layering_quality: Excellent — fine lines do not obscure lower layers
emotional_association: Delicacy, refinement, the spider-web of thought, lyrical precision within chaos
color_stacking: Very fine lines readable against any background
variation_potential: High — stream width varies with container hole size and paint viscosity

name: Impasto Push
technique: Very thick undiluted paint applied with stiff brush, palette knife, or painting knife and physically pushed, twisted, or dragged across canvas; paint may be mixed directly on canvas surface; thick enough to hold brush marks and knife strokes in relief
physics: Non-Newtonian paste behavior; thixotropic — paint viscosity decreases under shear then recovers; paint yield stress must be overcome to flow; marks preserved as paint gels; film thickness can exceed 10mm; compression waves in paint visible as ridges
control: Medium — tool direction controlled, exact texture form emergent
speed: Slow to medium — thick paint resists rapid movement
visual_result: Bold three-dimensional marks; visible brush/knife strokes preserved in relief; complex micro-texture; paint may crack when dry if too thick; highlights from directional raking light; peaks and valleys create strong shadow play
layering_quality: Poor — colors mix unavoidably in wet impasto; only possible over completely dry layers
emotional_association: Physicality, material substance, struggle, the weight of matter, the body working against resistance
color_stacking: Colors mix in the push; swirling color blends characteristic of impasto work; color at peak different from color in valley (thickness variation)
texture_quality: Maximum — defines the extreme of painterly texture; museum works visibly sculptural
variation_potential: Very High — tool type, paint consistency, layer thickness, mixing on canvas
scale_capability: Any scale; intimate scale particularly powerful
duration: Long — thick paint requires sustained physical effort
cleanup_effort: High — thick paint difficult to remove from tools
